title: Reinforcement Learning for Optimal Experiment Design in Parameter Identification of Mechatronic Systems
abstract: 信息充分的激励信号对于机电系统精准辨识至关重要，但传统系统辨识方法依赖专家知识和人工设计信号以保障硬件安全约束，这限制了方法的普适性。本文提出一种强化学习智能体，可在Quanser Aero 2实验平台上通过学习最优激励信号，同时通过奖励塑造机制自主满足安全约束。在10组独立训练种子上的评估表明，所提出的综合智能体在全部三个辨识参数上均实现了具有竞争力的估计精度，不仅优于传统基线方法，且仅产生0.75%的安全违规率。

Reinforcement Learning for Optimal Experiment Design in Parameter Identification of Mechatronic Systems
Julian Langschwert
Georg Schäfer
Jakob Rehrl
Stefan Huber
Simon Hirländer
Abstract
Informative excitation signals are critical for accurate system identification of mechatronic systems, yet classical
system identification
(
SI
) approaches require expert knowledge and hand-crafted signal design to respect hardware safety constraints, limiting their generalizability. We propose a
reinforcement learning
(
RL
) agent that learns optimal excitation signals for a Quanser Aero 2 testbed while autonomously enforcing safety constraints through reward shaping. Evaluated across 10 independent training seeds, our comprehensive agent achieves competitive estimation accuracy across all three identified parameters, outperforming classical baselines while incurring only
0.75
%
0.75\%
safety violations.
1
Introduction
\Ac
si is a fundamental process for modeling complex dynamics
[
3
]
. Field experts with extensive system and domain knowledge are required for tasks such as model selection,
optimal experimental design
(
OED
), data collection, and parameter estimation. A complicating factor is that many
cyber-physical systems
have strict hardware or safety constraints that need to be respected when designing these experiments.
Current
SI
strategies rely on standard open-loop excitation signals such as
pseudo-random binary sequences
(
PRBS
) or frequency sweeps
[
3
]
. While highly effective, their application necessitates extensive manual tuning to simultaneously maximize information yield
[
4
]
and respect strict physical safety constraints.
Recent works demonstrate that
RL
can successfully automate
OED
; for instance, by optimizing input sequences for parameter estimation in Lithium-ion batteries
[
2
]
, and by utilizing D-optimality-guided rewards to efficiently calibrate a multi-DOF rehabilitation robot
[
1
]
. Building upon this concept, this work-in-progress paper contributes an
RL
agent that learns an excitation signal for a Quanser Aero 2 testbed in a 1-
degree-of-freedom
(
DOF
) configuration while minimizing parameter estimation error and autonomously respecting hardware safety constraints through reward shaping.
2
Methodology and Experimental Setup

Parameter Estimation:
For parameter identification, a discrete
AutoRegressive with eXogenous inputs
(
ARX
) model coupled with a
recursive least-squares
(
RLS
) estimator is utilized, recovering the physical parameters via a nonlinear inverse mapping at the end of the episode.
Reward Formulation:
The objective of the agent is to maximize the expected discounted return. The reward function is designed to balance parameter estimation quality with strict hardware safety, quantified by:
1. Stepwise Safety Penalty:
The Quanser Aero 2 has a manufacturer-specified operating range of
±
45
∘
\pm 45^{\circ}
. To account for system inertia and avoid mechanical stress at the physical boundaries, we define a conservative critical limit
θ
lim
=
40
∘
\theta_{\lim}=40^{\circ}
. Furthermore, a warning threshold of
θ
warn
=
30
∘
\theta_{\mathrm{warn}}=30^{\circ}
was determined empirically to provide a sufficient
10
∘
10^{\circ}
buffer zone, allowing the agent to learn to decelerate before breaching the limit. The agent receives a quadratic penalty inside this warning zone and a severe penalty for breaching the critical limit:

(3)
2. Terminal Estimation Reward:
Crucially, the
RL
agent is trained entirely in a simulation environment. During this training phase, the physical parameters are based on nominal estimates from prior baseline experiments. In simulation, these parameters serve as the known ground truth
p
i
∗
p_{i}^{*}
for the reward calculation, allowing the agent to learn an optimal policy offline that can subsequently be deployed on real hardware where the parameters are truly unknown. Because the estimation quality is evaluated at the end of the trajectory, the agent receives a sparse terminal reward based on the relative error between the
RLS
-estimated parameters
p

(4)
Training and Evaluation Setup.
The
RL
agent uses a
proximal policy optimization
(
PPO
) algorithm from Stable-Baselines3 with two hidden layers of 64 neurons each. To ensure robust learning and address the sim-to-real gap, the true physical parameters are randomized by
±
30
%
\pm 30\%
of their nominal values at the start of each episode. To thoroughly evaluate the proposed approach, four different
RL
agents were trained. Three specialized agents were trained to prioritize the estimation accuracy of a single specific parameter (
J
p
J_{p}
,
K
p
​
u
K_{pu}
, or
D
p
D_{p}
). While the underlying
RLS
algorithm always estimates all three parameters, this modification was achieved by changing the terminal reward to penalize only the relative error of the targeted parameter. A fourth, comprehensive agent was trained to jointly estimate all three parameters using the full terminal reward formulation. Each agent was trained with ten independent random seeds to ensure robustness to training stochasticity.
Baseline Comparison:
To evaluate the
RL
approach, the trained agents are benchmarked against three classical open-loop signals, all restricted to a conservative
±
7.2
\pm 7.2
V amplitude to ensure hardware safety. The first is a naive
random
excitation drawn uniformly from
[
−
7.2
​
V
,
7.2
​
V
]
[-7.2\,\mathrm{V},7.2\,\mathrm{V}]
at each step. The second is a persistently exciting
PRBS
switching between discrete
±
7.2
\pm 7.2
V states. The third is a
Chirp
signal sweeping from
0.05
0.05
to
2
2
Hz, providing broadband excitation tailored to the pitch dynamics. For a strictly fair comparison, all methods are evaluated at a
10
10
Hz sampling rate over the exact same episode length using the identical least-squares algorithm.
3
Results and Discussion
Training Stability.
As detailed in Table
1

N
seeds
N_{\mathrm{seeds}}
column indicates that targeting
D

D_{p}
makes training less stable. While the parameter-specific agents

(

) achieved full convergence,

(

) converged in 9/10 seeds, and
RL
(all) in only 8/10. Specifically, two of the comprehensive agents collapsed to a zero-output policy, highlighting the challenge of jointly optimizing for all three parameters simultaneously. Furthermore, the observed
standard deviations
are generally large. For the
J
p
J_{p}
estimation of the
RL
(
K

​

K_{pu}
) agent, the
SD
entirely exceeds the mean, underlining the need for more stable training procedures in future work.
Estimation Accuracy and Safety Trade-off.
The
RL
(
D
p
D_{p}
) agent outperforms all other policies across
all three
parameters, suggesting that successfully exciting the most difficult parameter yields a trajectory rich enough to identify the remaining ones. However, the
RL
(
D
p
D_{p}
) agent’s regular safety violations of 151/900 evaluations (a rate of approximately 16.8%) render it unsuitable for deployment on physical hardware. Consequently, the comprehensive agent emerges as the practically preferred policy, achieving
D
p
D_{p}
error of 198% versus 495% for the best classical baseline while safely operating within physical limits, incurring only 0.75% safety violations.
Damping Identification Challenges.
Despite these results, all methods continue to struggle with accurately identifying the viscous damping coefficient
D
p
D_{p}
. This is consistent with ill-conditioning in the discrete
ARX
regressor matrix:
D
p
D_{p}
contributes weakly to the regression signal relative to
J
p
J_{p}
and
K
p
​
u
K_{pu}
, making its contribution difficult to isolate regardless of excitation quality.
Actuator Range and Comparison Validity.
A key advantage of the
RL
approach is its ability to dynamically utilize the full
±
24
\pm 24
V range while respecting safety constraints through reward shaping. Classical baselines required manual restrictions to
±
7.2
\pm 7.2
V to prevent safety violations. Yet, even with this conservative amplitude, the
PRBS
baseline still exceeded the physical limit twice. While this disparity
in operating voltage limits direct comparison, it simultaneously demonstrates the core contribution of
RL
-based
OED
: rather than requiring conservative hand-crafted amplitude limits, the agent learns to safely exploit the available actuator range to maximize information yield.

Conclusion and Future Work.
This work demonstrates that
RL
can successfully automate optimal experimental design for mechatronic systems by learning excitation signals that outperform classical baselines while autonomously enforcing hardware safety constraints. However, accurately identifying
D
p
D_{p}
remains an open challenge across all methods. To address this, we will replace the discrete ARX-RLS estimator with a batch
Nonlinear Least Squares
(
NLS
) approach operating on the continuous-time trajectory, avoiding ill-conditioning of the inverse mapping. In parallel, we plan to replace the sparse terminal reward with a dense, step-wise reward formulated around the Fisher Information Matrix, which directly measures the information content of the excitation trajectory. Further simulation work will refine the safety formulation by normalizing the penalty with respect to episode length
T
T
, investigate broader parameter randomization bounds to improve policy robustness, and conduct a formal optimization of the classical baseline parameters for a more rigorous comparison. Once the simulation framework is mature, the learned policies will be validated on the physical Quanser Aero 2 hardware to evaluate sim-to-real transfer.
Acknowledgments.
Financial support for this study was provided by the Christian Doppler Research Association (CDG) through the Josef Ressel Centre for Intelligent and Secure Industrial Automation, the corresponding WISS Co-project of Land Salzburg, and by the European Interreg project BA0100172 AI4GREEN.

Informative excitation signals are critical for accurate system identification of mechatronic systems, yet classical system identification (SI) approaches require expert knowledge and hand-crafted signal design to respect hardware safety constraints, limiting their generalizability. We propose a reinforcement learning (RL) agent that learns optimal excitation signals for a Quanser Aero 2 testbed while autonomously enforcing safety constraints through reward shaping. Evaluated across 10 independent training seeds, our comprehensive agent achieves competitive estimation accuracy across all three identified parameters, outperforming classical baselines while incurring only 0.75% safety violations.
